html:
YOC AG (LSE:0NN5 / primary Xetra:YOC): Small, Punished, and Still Inflecting

YOC is not the clean 80% gross-margin software story the old bull case implied. The audited 2025 gross margin was 42.4%, down from 47.3%, because this is an ad-trading platform with publisher payouts, agency rebates, data costs, server costs, and traffic-quality complexity. That matters. But the better thesis is that the stock has already been repriced for structural impairment while the actual business still grew revenue, stayed cash-generative, and guided to a 2026 profit recovery.

The reset was severe. FY2025 revenue rose 6% to EUR 37.1 million, but EBITDA fell to EUR 2.4 million and net income swung to EUR -0.4 million after a weak German Q4, margin pressure, FX headwinds, and one-off platform and open-market experiments that management says were discontinued or temporary. International revenue was the tell: it grew 15%, while Germany fell only 1% despite a weak local display and video market. Operating cash flow still held at EUR 3.8 million, capex was EUR 2.6 million, and year-end cash was EUR 4.1 million with EUR 1.5 million of unused credit lines. Those are not distress numbers. They are the numbers of a small ad-tech platform that overpromised, missed, and got abandoned. YOC FY2025 results

At the current official Xetra quote of EUR 6.68, YOC’s equity value is only EUR 23.2 million on 3.476 million shares. Against management’s FY2026 guidance of EUR 39.0 million to EUR 41.0 million revenue, EUR 3.0 million to EUR 4.5 million EBITDA, and breakeven to EUR 1.5 million net income, the whole market cap is just 0.58x forward sales and 6.2x midpoint guided EBITDA. Netting only the disclosed EUR 4.1 million cash, the cash-adjusted equity value is roughly EUR 19.1 million, or 5.1x midpoint EBITDA and 4.2x the high end. That is cheap even before assuming a real recovery in 2027. Current share data, 2026 guidance

The variant perception is not “YOC is a perfect SaaS business.” It is that the market is extrapolating a margin accident into a permanent impairment. The 2025 issue was not demand destruction. Revenue grew, 9M revenue was up 13%, Q3 revenue was up 18%, and the miss came from gross-profit pressure and a bad Q4. If gross margin recovers even partway toward the 2024 level, the operating leverage is large because YOC already carries the people, platform, and market footprint. A 4 to 5 percentage point gross-margin recovery on EUR 40 million-plus of revenue is EUR 1.6 million to EUR 2.0 million of incremental gross profit before any sales growth. That is the difference between “tiny loss-making ad-tech stock” and “EUR 4 million to EUR 5 million EBITDA compounder.”

The business also has a plausible forward product cycle. VIS.X is built for high-impact brand advertising across open marketplace, private marketplace, and guaranteed/direct channels. Its differentiation is not generic banners, but large-format and interactive units that premium publishers can monetize at higher attention value. In 2025 YOC expanded VIS.X Identity Intelligence with proprietary audience segments, reducing reliance on third-party data, and in March 2026 it added Connected TV into its AI-powered video product. That matters because CTV lets YOC sell the same “high-impact across screens” concept into TV-budget migration, not just mobile and desktop display. Early CTV campaigns cited by the company include eBay, Pandora, Mondelez, and Electrolux. VIS.X annual report discussion, CTV launch

The retail context is also useful. In August 2025, when the stock was around EUR 14.30, a Reddit value-investing post still pitched YOC as a dramatic bargain using the old 2025 guidance and a DCF value above EUR 30. That thesis was too clean and got hit by reality. Today the stock is less than half that level, the guidance miss is known, and investor confidence has been flushed out. The upside no longer depends on heroic 15% growth for years. It depends on management delivering the already guided 2026 EBITDA recovery and showing that 2025’s gross-margin compression was not the new normal. Reddit retail write-up

The biggest risk is that 2025 was not temporary. If agency rebates, publisher payouts, identity costs, and server costs keep rising, YOC may be a low-margin intermediary rather than a scalable platform. Independent SSPs also face pressure from Google, The Trade Desk supply-path optimization, direct publisher deals, and advertiser budget concentration in walled gardens. Liquidity is another real risk: this is a microcap, and the LSE 0NN5 line is less relevant than the German listing for actual price discovery. Founder/management ownership helps alignment, but the company is still small enough that execution errors show up immediately in the P&L.

The setup is asymmetric because the price already assumes a lot of that. If management simply hits the midpoint of 2026 guidance, the stock is cheap. If EBITDA returns to the 2024 level over the next 18 to 24 months, it is very cheap. If CTV, AI optimization, proprietary audience data, and international markets turn YOC back into a double-digit grower, the equity can re-rate violently because the market cap is only EUR 23 million.

Bear case: EUR 4.00, down 40% from EUR 6.68. Revenue stalls near EUR 37 million to EUR 38 million, gross margin stays around 40% to 42%, EBITDA settles near EUR 1.5 million, and the market values YOC at a distressed 6x EBITDA plus partial cash credit.

Base case: EUR 13.00, up 95%. Revenue reaches the low EUR 40 millions in 2026 and mid EUR 40 millions in 2027, EBITDA normalizes to roughly EUR 5 million, and the market applies 8x EBITDA plus cash. That is still a small-cap and liquidity discount, not a premium multiple.

Bull case: EUR 22.00, up 229%. International growth continues, CTV becomes material, gross margin recovers toward the mid-to-high 40s, and EBITDA reaches EUR 8 million to EUR 9 million within a few years. At 9x to 10x EBITDA plus cash, YOC becomes a EUR 75 million-plus equity value, still modest for a profitable European ad-tech platform with proprietary formats and premium publisher relationships.
